(9) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Picking an Image Host
This post is about picking an image host.

Quick context on why you need to pick one: Hexo doesn't have built-in image uploading, so you need somewhere to put the images that go in your blog posts. Without an image host, you can't put any images in your blog. If you're only writing text-only posts though, it doesn't matter.
Here are a few image hosts to start with (I'll call them "hosts" from here on):
PPT URL shortener — image crop tool!
That's a short list — mostly because I don't use many hosts myself. I mainly use imgur. When using free hosts, watch out for:
Some hosts only keep your image alive for 30 days, 90 days, etc.
Some hosts don't let you grab a direct image link.
Are there bandwidth or view limits?
Some hosts cap how many times an image can be loaded.
Free hosts will always come with some inconvenience and limits — free things are often the most expensive in the long run.
On top of that, a lot of free hosts don't let you organise images into categories after upload, which makes things kind of annoying. If you're just writing notes for yourself, consider

— it's free, doesn't cap bandwidth, doesn't limit storage time, and supports hotlinking, so it works fine for blog images. Best of all, you can organise into albums and choose whether they're public. Even hidden albums still let you grab image links that display normally:

imgur is solid, but for serious tutorial articles with a lot of images, I'd recommend a paid host. imgur's terms say they won't delete your images unless you're hosting something violent or illegal, but one of their rules states they don't allow hotlinking — i.e. directly copying the image URL. They want you to use their Embed Unit instead, which looks like this:
The code usually looks like this:
Cartoon Physics
Compared to the usual way of writing an image in Markdown:
So yeah, way more involved. imgur's
covers how to use Embed Unit.
Quietly admitting I've been hotlinking the whole time.

So if the images in your post matter, I'd recommend a paid host. Personally I use Firebase Storage for uploads. Firebase Storage gives you 1 GB of outbound bandwidth per day and lets you store about 15,000 images (at 2MB each), which is plenty for a small blog. Worth a
If your blog is pulling more than 1GB of traffic per day, you can probably afford to pay — that's already a lot of traffic, lol.
So that's it for now. There are tons of image host options out there — I've just covered the ones I've personally used or am currently using.
